subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly in tables and graphs with labels, units and error bars.
A full suite of averages, standard deviations and sample calculations is provided, supporting transparency.
The comparison between experimental and theoretical trends is effectively discussed, showing good interpretation.
weaknesses: Serious errors in uncertainty propagation (double-counting percentage terms) and an incorrect radius formula undermine the accuracy of processed results.
End-correction and temperature uncertainties are mentioned qualitatively but not included in quantitative propagation.
Inconsistent units (mL vs m^3) and a minor OCR artifact further detract from precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although presentation is clear, the significant inaccuracies in calculations and omissions in uncertainty treatment warrant a mark in the lower mid band (3/6).

strengths: The research question is clearly stated within a well-defined Helmholtzâresonator context.
Methodology is detailed and reproducible, with apparatus, volumes, uncertainties and environmental controls specified.
Justifications for step sizes, trial counts and controls show strong awareness of relevance and sufficiency.
weaknesses: The derivation of the full Helmholtzâresonator formula is not explicitly linked to the simpler wave equation; a brief bridging statement would improve clarity.
Minor procedural details (e.g. straw orientation) are omitted, requiring trivial but necessary clarifications for full reproducibility.
Context, while specific, could be deepened with reference to a recent or novel application to achieve the very top band.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the design is robust and specific, meeting most top-band descriptors but missing a few explanatory details. This justifies a solid 4/6 rather than the maximum.

strengths: The conclusion succinctly restates the inverseâsquareâroot relationship with quantitative slopes and RÂ² values.
It draws direct links between data and hypothesis, demonstrating consistency with the analysis.
Relevant comparisons to accepted Helmholtz-resonator theory are cited to interpret discrepancies.
weaknesses: Discussion of how uncertainties influence the confidence in the conclusion is absent and would strengthen the argument.
Numerical uncertainties for slopes and intercepts are not specified, limiting quantitative justification.
A deeper discussion of alternative models or temperature dependence could further contextualize findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well-justified and closely aligned with the data and theory, meriting a high mark (5/6), with only minor omissions preventing a full 6.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (airflow inconsistency, limited volume range, bottle geometry) are clearly identified.
Suggested improvements (mechanical pump, finer increments, bespoke formula) are realistic and directly related to reducing errors.
The evaluation shows awareness of major error sources and practical mitigation strategies.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each identified weakness on overall uncertainty is not quantified.
Improvements are described rather than explained in terms of how much they would reduce systematic or random errors.
A brief quantitative appraisal of expected gains from each improvement would move this into the top band.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets the mid-band descriptor: it describes specific issues and realistic improvements but lacks quantitative explanation of impact, justifying 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â âInvestigating the relationship between the volume of water in a 591 mL plastic bottle and its resonance frequencyâ â is explicitly stated, and the introduction devotes several paragraphs to the Helmholtz-resonator model, derives the governing formula, and links the proposed manipulation (water volume â air volume) to expected frequency changes.  Relevant physical theory (standing waves, adiabatic compression, speed of sound) is cited with sources, giving the reader a precise experimental and theoretical framework.  This satisfies the descriptor of âspecific and appropriate context,â though the context is not exceptionally novel; therefore the strand sits securely in the top band but is best rewarded with 5 rather than 6 marks.

Uncertainties are treated at several stages (instrumental Â±0.2 Hz, graduated-cylinder Â±0.1 mL, propagated standard error, error bars).  However, propagation to theoretical frequencies is simplistic, and percentage uncertainty combinations are sometimes incorrect (e.g., double-counting volume).  End-correction and temperature uncertainty are mentioned qualitatively but not propagated.  This demonstrates consideration with notable omissions, matching the 3-4 band; awarding 3 reflects the frequency and magnitude of the lapses.

The student explains why 100 mL steps and five water levels were chosen, discusses the need for 10 trials to reduce random error, and justifies room-temperature constancy.  They recognise instrumental resolution, end corrections, and airflow consistency as methodological factors and attempt to quantify them.  The rationale shows awareness of *relevance* (direct impact on the research question) and *sufficiency* (range and repeatability) of the data.  Explanations are present but not deeply analytical (e.g., no quantitative power analysis), so 5 marks within the top band are appropriate.

Weaknesses are identified (airflow inconsistency, limited volume range, bottle shape versus ideal resonator).  These are described specifically but the *relative impact* on final uncertainty is not quantified; terms like âlikely influencedâ are qualitative.  The response meets the descriptor for 3-4 marks; 3 marks reflect largely descriptive rather than explanatory treatment.

The student lists all apparatus, provides step-by-step instructions (distance of 10.0 Â± 0.1 cm from microphone, 100 mL increments, 10 repeats, Phyphox for FFT read-out, room temperature and noise conditions, preliminary-trial adjustments).  Volumes, diameters and neck length are specified with uncertainties; even environmental controls are noted.  A competent peer could reproduce the investigation with only trivial clarifications (e.g., brand of bottle).  This meets the requirement of an unambiguous, reproducible method, meriting the 5-6 band; minor omissions (no specification of straw angle or air-flow speed) justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

A full set of averages, standard deviations, and linearised datasets is produced, and regressions yield RÂ² = 1.  Calculations generally give plausible values that fit the theoretical trend, indicating correct arithmetic.  Nevertheless, the mis-written radius formula and the simplistic uncertainty propagation introduce inaccuracy in the *theoretical* reference line.  Processing is thus âmostly appropriate and accurateâ with some significant but non-fatal errors, justifying 4 marks in the 3-4 band.

Improvements such as using a mechanical air pump, finer volume increments, and deriving a bespoke resonance formula are realistic and relevant.  Nonetheless, they are *stated/described* rather than fully *explained* with clear linkage to how much each would reduce error or extend conclusions.  This positions the work solidly in the 3-4 band; 3 marks are awarded.

Raw and processed data are given in neatly formatted tables with units and uncertainties; significant figures are consistent.  Processing steps (averaging, standard deviation, error propagation) are described and sample calculations shown.  Graphs include labelled axes, error bars and regression equations.  The presentation is both *clear* (easy to follow) and *precise* (numerical detail), fully meeting the 5-6 band; minor typographical slips (radius formula mis-typed) slightly detract, so 5 marks are awarded.

The student compares experimental slopes and intercepts with those predicted by the Helmholtz formula, discusses end correction and airflow factors, and references accepted theory of standing waves.  Sources are cited, and the comparison is used to interpret discrepancies.  Although deeper quantitative discussion of Î³, temperature dependence, or alternative models would strengthen it, the comparison is still ârelevant and justified,â earning 5 marks in the 5-6 band.

The conclusion restates the linear inverse-square-root relationship, cites numerical slopes and RÂ² values, and explicitly links these to the hypothesis and data.  It discusses how decreasing air volume produces higher frequency with supporting quantitative evidence.  Consistency with analysis is strong; however, the student does not tackle every outlier or uncertainty in depth, therefore 5 (not 6) marks are appropriate within the top band.

annotations.comment: The percent uncertainty equation applies the volume term twice. Clarify which measurements contribute each uncertainty to avoid double-counting.
Enhance the caption in Table 3 by specifying column headings (e.g., âEmptyâspace volumeâ and â1/âVolumeâ) for clearer data presentation.
Detailed description of averaging and errorâcalculation procedures enhances transparency and supports the reliability of the results.
When combining standard error and instrumental error, consider also contributions from temperature and endâcorrection uncertainties to reflect total error.
Weaknesses are described descriptively, but the relative impact of each source of error is not quantified. Explain which errors most affect the final uncertainty.

The research question and experimental context are clearly stated, linking water volume changes to resonance frequency via the Helmholtz-resonator model. This provides a strong, specific background for the investigation.
Consider framing the introduction around a more novel application or recent study to deepen context and engage the reader with current research trends.
The introduction presents $$f_0 = c/\lambda$$ but stops short of explicitly deriving the Helmholtz-resonator form. Add a sentence linking this to the full resonator formula to guide the reader.
When introducing $$L_{\rm neck}=\lambda/4$$, explicitly note any endâcorrection assumptions so readers understand how the neck length is adjusted.
In the derivation section, reference the adiabatic assumption earlier to justify introducing $$\gamma$$ when relating pressure and volume changes.
The step-by-step derivation of the resonant frequency is logically structured and clearly connects Newtonâs Second Law to SHM, demonstrating strong methodological reasoning.
An OCR artifact shows a matrix symbol in the unit for crossâsectional area. Replace with clear notation (m^2) to avoid confusion.
Specify the angle and method of airflow (e.g., straw orientation) in step 3 to ensure reproducibility of the blowing technique.
Including safety, ethical, and environmental considerations demonstrates thorough planning and respect for experimental constraints.
The preliminary trial justification for 100 mL increments and limiting range to 0â400 mL is well argued, balancing precision and practicality.
The radius formula $$r=\frac{D}{2\pi}$$ is incorrect. The radius should be $$D/2$$. This error propagates through area and theoretical frequency values.
Uncertainty propagation is handled via simple addition of percent terms. Use quadrature (root-sum-of-squares) for combining independent relative uncertainties more accurately.
In the theoretical data table, include the uncertainty contribution from endâcorrection length to reflect all significant sources.
The discussion effectively compares experimental and theoretical trends and attributes discrepancies to systematic errors, demonstrating solid interpretation.
The conclusion restates the correlation but omits discussion of how uncertainties affect its validity. Address how error bars influence confidence in the result.
The graph caption uses mL for emptyâspace volume but processing uses mÂ³. Ensure units are consistent between text, tables, and figures.
In the section discussing slopes and intercepts, include numeric values and uncertainties to quantitatively justify how the experimental fit compares to theory.
The final conclusion succinctly links experimental findings to theory, reinforcing the hypothesis with quantitative evidence and clear language.
The evaluation section outlines strengths clearly, highlighting variable control and theoretical grounding as notable merits.
Each suggested improvement is realistic, but explain how implementing a mechanical pump or finer increments quantitatively reduces random or systematic errors.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
